category_summary:
**OpenAI's** custom silicon dominated the day. The company and president **Greg Brockman** unveiled **Jalapeño**, a [from-scratch LLM-inference chip](/?date=2026-06-25&category=social#item-d5c9aff2fe82) built with **Broadcom** over nine months, signaling full-stack vertical integration. The flagship announcement drew massive reach (3.8M views), with newsletters and commentators amplifying claims of strong inference performance powering ChatGPT, Codex, and agentic workloads.

- **John Carmack** delivered a widely-shared [reflection on early-career mistakes](/?date=2026-06-25&category=social#item-192a01f8b2ae) from the Quake era—over-ambition, startup-intensity burnout, and team management—resonating broadly with engineers.
- **Andrej Karpathy** [clarified his "org-level harness" concept](/?date=2026-06-25&category=social#item-13760a67efd2), framing it as an enterprise-grade, multiplayer agentic-work paradigm rather than a simple Slack bot, sparking debate after critics misread the title.
- Agentic tooling threads ran deep: **Harrison Chase (LangChain)** detailed a [LangSmith "sleep-time compute" pattern](/?date=2026-06-25&category=social#item-2e73bfbc806e) for agent memory, while **Allie Miller** described [per-teammate Claude triage channels](/?date=2026-06-25&category=social#item-29dd81a59671).

- **Nathan Lambert** anchored the open-model discussion, praising **GLM**'s wins while [flagging its brittle, jagged behavior](/?date=2026-06-25&category=social#item-63c676e73fef) versus closed models and [arguing it pressures frontier-lab margins](/?date=2026-06-25&category=social#item-cde18afe71b4). **swyx** noted **Zai's** [January IPO and GLM overtaking](/?date=2026-06-25&category=social#item-2070adf6ad8f) **DeepSeek**.
- **Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex)** benchmarked the newly-released **Mistral OCR** [on ParseBench, finding it competitive](/?date=2026-06-25&category=social#item-ef95cfc55e88) for its price.
- **NVIDIA** [detailed NeMo AutoModel's MoE training](/?date=2026-06-25&category=social#item-0bbd66ae84fb) on **Hugging Face** Transformers v5. **Gary Marcus** [amplified the escalating AI-bubble debate](/?date=2026-06-25&category=social#item-095489a6c0be) via a Bloomberg quote.

items.title:
We’ve designed and built our first AI chip: Jalapeño.

Designed from the ground up by OpenAI and bro...
items.content:
We’ve designed and built our first AI chip: Jalapeño.

Designed from the ground up by OpenAI and brought to production with @Broadcom, Jalapeño is purpose-built for the LLM workloads powering ChatGPT, Codex, the API, and future agentic products.

Chips are foundational to the AI economy. Building our own expands our full-stack platform from products to models to infrastructure, and will help us scale intelligence, serve more people, and expand access to AI.

items.title:
There are a few things that I look back on as my mistakes in the early days.

Quake was overly ambit...
items.content:
There are a few things that I look back on as my mistakes in the early days.

Quake was overly ambitious technically. We could have done all the great multiplayer and modding work inside a Doom++ engine, allowing the designers to work with a more stable base instead of rug-pulling everything out from underneath them a couple times. The follow up game could have then brought in full 6DOF environments and characters.

I pushed everyone too hard. I didn’t appreciate how maturing companies need more slack, and that running people at startup intensity constantly will wear them out. Quake was also where I really had to accept my personal limits. I was working pretty much as hard as humanly possible, and I was still slipping past my goal points.

On all of the founders’ shoulders, our original corporate stock arrangement and buy/sell agreement was a mistake, and resulted in bad incentives. We wanted to ensure that all ownership rested in the hands of people working hard on current projects, but the Silicon Valley standard approach of vesting stock would have worked out better.

One real problem that I don’t accept the blame for is that we were insisting that level designers be not just game designers, but also have strong visual design esthetics. They needed to make things that not only played well, but looked awesome, and it got more challenging as the technology provided a richer palette. Romero covered that well, which set our company expectations early on.

We should have figured out how to pair up artists and designers earlier, but there was infighting among the designers, and the ones that could manage the visuals were happy to disparage the ones that couldn’t.

Sorry, Sandy.

items.title: Another quick lecture -- I've been asked many times for prereq's to my book and what you should know...
items.content:
Another quick lecture -- I've been asked many times for prereq's to my book and what you should know, so built a little lecture (with GLM 5.2) to cover some more basics.

Topics include:

00:00 Introduction & Course Prerequisites
01:37 Language Models Overview
02:47 The LM Head
04:29 Softmax & Log-Probabilities
06:13 Anatomy of an LM Training Example
06:37 Computing LLM Probabilities (+Phoebe the Dog)
09:52 Three Common Masks in Post-Training
11:03 A Small Decoding Review
12:14 Training an LM: Cross-Entropy
13:23 Optimization & Fine-Tuning
13:55 Pretraining to Midtraining to SFT Pipeline
15:25 Probability Essentials: KL Divergence & Entropy
19:36 Sigmoid & Pairwise Likelihood
20:29 Reinforcement Learning Framing (MDP)
22:28 Transitioning Tools into Post-Training
23:12 Recommended Resources & Wrap-Up

Happy learning and I'm still taking questions from during the course for Q&A videos.

items.title:
🧠LangSmith Engine as Sleep Time Compute

Memory for agents is often described as “sleep time compute...
items.content:
🧠LangSmith Engine as Sleep Time Compute

Memory for agents is often described as “sleep time compute” or “dreaming”

This involves running a background process to analyze agent trajectories and update a memory store

Today, we show how to do that in LangSmith:

1. Trace all agent trajectories to LangSmith
2. Use LangSmith Engine to analyze and suggest memory updates
3. Store and update memory in Context Hub

Tutorial: https://t.co/sFEtTUueLO

items.summary: Hwchase17 explains a LangSmith Engine tutorial implementing sleep-time compute for agent memory by tracing trajectories, analyzing them, and updating a memory store via Context Hub.

items.title:
We benchmarked Mistral OCR against other frontier and open-weight models on ParseBench 📊

For a mode...
items.content:
We benchmarked Mistral OCR against other frontier and open-weight models on ParseBench 📊

For a model at its price point, it is quite competitive! 
- It wins on semantic formatting - understanding strikethroughs, superscripts/subscripts, title hierarchy, links
- It is competitive on content faithfulness (reading order + hallucinations + omissions) and visual grounding (bounding boxes) 
- It does ok on tables and doesn't really have chart capabilities.

Of course, some of the frontier models + OCR providers like Azure Doc Intelligence + AWS Textract are a bit more expensive. 

Check out our full leaderboard on ParseBench: https://t.co/PWczfhp0OX

items.title: @salomon_diei The basic idea is easy and v0 is a hackathon project. The product here is a lot closer...
items.content: @salomon_diei The basic idea is easy and v0 is a hackathon project. The product here is a lot closer to *it actually works*, for enterprise grade deployments, and after quite a bit of internal experimentation and iteration. It’s kind of hard to describe other than (per the post) it’s writing majority of code, it’s deeply integrated, multiplayer, and it starts to feel like everyone is a manager. So I understand it looks easy to dismiss on quick reading but it’s not some LLM Q&A with RAG over Slack, it’s not even OpenClaw adjacent, it’s a different way of working entirely, for people and teams. I work from Slack now.

items.title: The rise of MoE models introduced new challenges in training, and @huggingface's Transformers v5 bro...
items.content:
The rise of MoE models introduced new challenges in training, and @huggingface's Transformers v5 brought first-class support for solving them.

Now, NeMo AutoModel builds on top of v5. Part of the NeMo framework for building models at scale, NeMo AutoModel brings optimizations to a broad set of model families through support for Expert Parallelism, DeepEP, and TransformerEngine kernels with a few lines of code.

We found NeMo AutoModel brings a 3.4 to 3.7x higher training throughput for popular MoE models. You can read more here: https://t.co/TNlBsKWwrJ

items.title:
The architecture of this new world model is one of the most interesting things I've seen lately:

Le...
items.content:
The architecture of this new world model is one of the most interesting things I've seen lately:

Let me first explain how most world models work:

They predict and render one frame at a time. If you are navigating in one of these worlds, and you look left, the model draws whatever looks right in the moment.

Every time you change your viewpoint, the model has to imagine what should be there again, so it's very common for these models to "forget" what's in the world. For example, if you put a toy on the table, look away, then look back, the toy might not be there anymore.

Tripo AI is releasing its Project Eden model, which works very differently:

The model builds the world first, and then renders it based on that map.

That map holds the real state of the world: the geometry, every object, where things are, what's already happened. The picture you see on screen gets generated from the map.

This architecture flips the whole thing. Now, you get the following:

1. The world stops forgetting. Leave, come back, and the toy is still on the table because it lives in the map, not in the last frame you saw.

2. You can edit the world, and those changes persist for anyone who enters later.

3. Multiple people and AI agents can coexist in the world and see it from different perspectives.

This is early research, but it's looking really promising. They just raised nearly $200M across two rounds to build it out.

Tripo will be at SIGGRAPH 2026 (July 19–23, Los Angeles Convention Center). If you work in 3D, embodied AI, simulation, or anything spatial, go connect with them there.

A spicy new (AI) chip has emerged. 

Jalapeño is OpenAI's first-gen, built-from-scratch chip for running LLMs, claiming performance per watt “substantially better than current state-of-the-art.”

Built in just 9 months with Broadcom, with OpenAI’s own AI models helping design and optimize the hardware.

items.title: We've provided some updated results on Mistral OCR that make use of the annotation feature for chart...
items.content:
We've provided some updated results on Mistral OCR that make use of the annotation feature for charts.

The overall score is ahead of GPT-5.5 and just behind Gemini 3.1 Pro, which is quite impressive for a model at its price range. 

It does a great job on content faithfulness, semantic formatting, and visual grounding. It does an ok job on tables and an ok job (though to be fair, non-zero) on charts. 

There's been some great advancements on visual understanding capabilities lately. See screenshot below for performance, will update the main benchmark page soon: https://t.co/PWczfhp0OX
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu shares updated Mistral OCR results using its annotation feature, scoring ahead of GPT-5.5 and just behind Gemini 3.1 Pro, strong on content faithfulness and semantic formatting at a low price point.

items.title:
@trq212 We’ve created channels for everyone on our team. 

If you need something from Susie, you go ...
items.content:
@trq212 We’ve created channels for everyone on our team. 

If you need something from Susie, you go to Susie’s channel and you ask for something.  

Susie’s Claude reviews the requests 24/7. 

If Susie’s Claude deems it low risk, it just executes the task and confirms in the thread. 

If higher risk, Susie’s Claude writes back in the chat that with emoji options (same as yours ✅🛑 etc), and does whatever Susie emoji reacts with. 

Ex: you post in Susie’s chat “find us 15min to update passwords across accounts” 

It’s great to have a Claude in slack. 

It’s better to not have the human on the bottleneck of small asks.

We think of it as “auto mode for actual teamwork”.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: AllieKMiller describes a workflow where each team member has a Slack channel with a personal Claude agent that triages requests by risk and executes or asks for emoji approval.
items.reasoning: Concrete, detailed real-world agentic AI workflow from a credible AI leader; novel team automation pattern, though low engagement on this reply.
items.summary_html: AllieKMiller describes a workflow where each team member has a Slack channel with a personal Claude agent that triages requests by risk and executes or asks for emoji approval.
items.content_html:
@trq212 We’ve created channels for everyone on our team.

If you need something from Susie, you go to Susie’s channel and you ask for something.

Susie’s Claude reviews the requests 24/7.

If Susie’s Claude deems it low risk, it just executes the task and confirms in the thread.

If higher risk, Susie’s Claude writes back in the chat that with emoji options (same as yours ✅🛑 etc), and does whatever Susie emoji reacts with.

Ex: you post in Susie’s chat “find us 15min to update passwords across accounts”

It’s great to have a Claude in slack.

It’s better to not have the human on the bottleneck of small asks.

We think of it as “auto mode for actual teamwork”.

items.title: Google was *literally* started by Larry and Sergey using Stanford resources without authorization. W...
items.content: Google was *literally* started by Larry and Sergey using Stanford resources without authorization. When Stanford found out what they were doing, they decided to figure out how to accommodate them without crashing internet traffic to the rest of the school. Then Stanford - amongst others - invested early into Google, and the rest is history. That kind of mindset created the Silicon Valley, but unfortunately is now all but extinct.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Recounts that Google began with Larry and Sergey using Stanford resources without authorization, and laments that this risk-taking Silicon Valley mindset is now fading.
items.reasoning: High-engagement, widely-resonant commentary on innovation culture and institutional risk tolerance, though historical rather than technical AI news.
items.summary_html: Recounts that Google began with Larry and Sergey using Stanford resources without authorization, and laments that this risk-taking Silicon Valley mindset is now fading.
items.content_html: Google was *literally* started by Larry and Sergey using Stanford resources without authorization. When Stanford found out what they were doing, they decided to figure out how to accommodate them without crashing internet traffic to the rest of the school. Then Stanford - amongst others - invested early into Google, and the rest is history. That kind of mindset created the Silicon Valley, but unfortunately is now all but extinct.

items.title:
Unlimited OCR is a great model on table parsing and understanding proper reading order.

However it ...
items.content:
Unlimited OCR is a great model on table parsing and understanding proper reading order.

However it does struggle a little on semantic formatting, charts (it does decent at bounding boxes).

Attaching the results below to compare against PaddleOCR-VL-1.6.

Overall it's still a great addition to the open-weight OCR model collection! 

Check the latest results on ParseBench: https://t.co/PWczfhp0OX

What happens when millions of AI agents start negotiating, transacting, and delegating to one another?

@weballergy joined our podcast with @fryrsquared to explore the rise of agentic economies – and how we can diversify agent decision-making to avoid AI groupthink.

Timecodes:
00:00 Intro
1:07 Defining AI agents
4:44 Agentic exploration in science and research
15:46 Delegation between agents
22:46 Agentic security and traps
29:31 Building an agentic economy

36:29 Distributed intelligence

items.content:
More smoke accumulating on a Fable 5 return... 

- References to Fable usage reportedly spotted in Claude Code v2.1.190 update log (H/T @synthwavedd):

Adding: "You've reached your Fable 5 limit".
Removing: "purchased separately from your plan", potentially pointing at coming usage changes

- Reports of references to Fable 5 also reappearing in Amazon Bedrock.

- Per WIRED, Trump admin is 'happier' in talks with Anthropic now that they are dealing with co-founder Tom Brown instead of Dario Amodei.

“Tom Brown is not being a weirdo like Dario and can actually engage." 

- Tech firm Legion filed the first lawsuit against the export control order, calling it "unlawful" and arguing "the government’s real motive was unlawful retaliation." (H/T @SophiaCai99)

items.content:
/5 OpenAI launches GPT-5.5-Cyber to scan, patch, and fix vulnerable code at scale.

GPT-5.5-Cyber is a specialized model that scored 85.6% on CyberGym (vs 81.8% for standard GPT-5.5). It is gated to verified defenders only, not open access.

Plugged into Codex, the new Codex Security plugin lets you run end-to-end security workflows without switching tools:

Since its research preview, Codex Security has scanned 30 million commits across 30,000+ codebases.

items.title:
Great feature but is this true “multi-player mode”? 

I’d say no, honestly. 

I know everyone is psy...
items.content:
Great feature but is this true “multi-player mode”? 

I’d say no, honestly. 

I know everyone is psyched for this, and I’m sure my team will get value from it, but what has been more valuable is having our personal AIs interact with each other and with the team. 

Essentially, we bring in our personal AI CoS to reply with *our* access points. 

Ex: my content lead can ask my personal AI CoS in a channel “hey, did Allie already set up time with the Mars Company?”

My AI CoS classifies that task into risk levels. 

If it’s low risk, my AI CoS replies on my behalf. 

If medium/high, it asks for permission or blocks the request. 

Our personal AI Chief of Staff can also collaborate with each other and cowork. 

That, to me, is multiplayer mode. 

This release is a new surface and is creating a new AI employee with its own memory and access points.

items.content:
@2plus2make5 @EricTopol @2plus2makes5 - unemployment: economists say there won't be mass unemployment due to AI. Don't listen to Dario, he is not an economist.  Talk to your Stanford colleague @erikbryn 
- inequality: that's a fiscal policy question, not an AI technology question. One can lament the growing inequalities in the US, but the solution is to reduce corruption not slow down AI (other countries are doing just fine, by the way). Inequality started growing in the US in the 1980s with Reagan. Nothing to do with AI (ask Erik).
- warfare: right now, the deployment of AI in drones is protecting liberal democracy in Europe against an authoritarian invader. Abandon at your own risk. I sure won't. 
- loss of meaning: Aristotle lamented the loss of meaning due to the increased prevalence of writing. Antique marathoners lamented the loss of meaning due to horse riding. AI won't cause any more loss of meaning than fire, iron, agriculture, writing, horses, books, engines, cinema, calculators, TV, computers, or the internet.

items.title: Most reasoning models that "think longer" on harder problems do so by writing out their reasoning as...
items.content:
Most reasoning models that "think longer" on harder problems do so by writing out their reasoning as text, one step at a time, which means training them needs worked examples that spell out those intermediate steps.

The alternative this paper builds on works differently: a transformer processes a problem into a set of internal activation vectors (one per input token, called the hidden state), and instead of decoding anything into words, the model feeds that hidden state back into the same network as input, over and over, with the original problem mixed back in each time.

Each pass rewrites the hidden state, so the reasoning accumulates inside those vectors across iterations rather than as written text, and the model can be trained on nothing more than problems paired with their final answers.

This setup raises two problems which authors address in the paper: how the model should decide on its own when to stop looping, and how to keep the repeated passes from destabilizing those activation vectors, since applying the same step many times behaves like a very deep network in which the signal degrades as it travels through.

https://t.co/xLSK9mmFT0
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov summarizes a paper on latent reasoning where a transformer iteratively feeds its hidden state back into itself, accumulating reasoning internally rather than as text, trainable on just problem-answer pairs, and addressing when to stop and how to stay stable.
items.reasoning: Solid technical explainer of an interesting reasoning approach, though modest reach.

items.title: A standard transformer keeps every past token in memory and looks back at them through attention whe...
items.content:
A standard transformer keeps every past token in memory and looks back at them through attention whenever it needs to, so it never has a reason to boil down its history into a small summary that it updates one step at a time the way a recurrent network does.

This freedom helps the model fit its training data, but it often leaves the model's internal representations disorganized, so the model fails on inputs different from those it trained on. The Manhattan taxi example considered in the paper demonstrates this: a model trained only on turn-by-turn routes learns to predict legal turns correctly, yet when researchers reconstruct the street map the model has effectively built from its own predictions, that map is full of impossible roads.

The authors of this paper from Microsoft Research introduce Next-Latent Prediction, an extra training objective that asks the model to predict its own next internal state (the compressed vector it forms after reading each token) from its current state plus the next token, using a small separate network to model that one-step transition.

The authors prove that a model satisfying this consistency condition is forced to compress its history into a "belief state," meaning a summary that holds everything needed to predict the future and nothing redundant, and they show this can be done without slowing the transformer's fast parallel training. The helper network also leaves inference untouched, since it is used only during training and discarded afterward.

Across taxi-route world modeling, an arithmetic reasoning game, graph path-finding, and full language-model pretraining at the 1.3-billion-parameter scale, the method yields more compact internal representations, better planning, and a useful side effect for speed: because the small transition network can be run forward on its own to guess several upcoming tokens, the model can verify multiple tokens at once and run inference up to about three times faster.

https://t.co/3KMFgr8hsu

items.title:
We should build a church for people who open-source their code so everyone can learn from it.

Here ...
items.content:
We should build a church for people who open-source their code so everyone can learn from it.

Here is the complete source code of a RAG assistant to navigate airline policies.

You get the complete source code and video from @lenadroid, walking you through everything she did (I'm linking to the video in the first comment below).

The fact that you can watch every engineering decision that Lena made when building this app is pure gold.

A few things you'll pick up from this:

• It uses LangChain for the retrieval pipeline
• It uses LangGraph for conversation state
• It stores embeddings in Postgres with pgvector
• It indexes documents to ground answers in the source text
• It uses Terraform to stand up the infrastructure

I'm linking to the video walkthrough and the source code below.

items.title: I’m reading an old collection of interconnected science fiction stories by Jerry Pournelle, written ...
items.content:
I’m reading an old collection of interconnected science fiction stories by Jerry Pournelle, written in the early 70s. His best books were later co-authored with Larry Niven, but it is still solid work in my favored “competence porn” genre, with entrepreneurs as protagonists.

It stands out to me that he was despairing for America when he wrote the stories. Things looked bad at the time, and his fiction projected it into the future. Social unrest, Vietnam, Watergate, economic recession, energy crisis, and for a patriotic space guy, abandoning Apollo.

The backdrop for the stories was that America was unfixable, which is, of course, a motivation to go to space in fiction, but I do think he was genuinely worried by what he saw around him.

But over the next decade, things got better, and Jerry had a front row seat for the rise of the technology sector, writing the Chaos Manor column in Byte magazine for many years. He also got to see the founding of SpaceX, a company straight out of a hard SF novel, and they re-flew a landed rocket shortly before he died.

Trends aren’t fate.

Bad situations can be fixed, and good ones still need to be defended.

RIP Jerry, I’m glad you got to see things turn around.

https://t.co/jdQVyVLevI

items.title:
I'm curious about the claims about persistent memory on this agent.

I've used a ton of different ag...
items.content:
I'm curious about the claims about persistent memory on this agent.

I've used a ton of different agents, and most forget everything as soon as you do something else.

Even ChatGPT and Claude feel very rudimentary when it comes to remembering past context (sometimes they forget important details or hold on to irrelevant information).

The lack of reliable persistent memory is one reason these agents aren't as good as promised.

If Vida fixed that, then I'm all ears.

items.reasoning: Thoughtful skeptical commentary on a real agent limitation from a popular practitioner with decent engagement.
items.summary_html: svpino questions persistent memory claims for an agent called Vida, noting most agents including ChatGPT and Claude forget context and that unreliable memory limits agent usefulness.
items.content_html:
I'm curious about the claims about persistent memory on this agent.

I've used a ton of different agents, and most forget everything as soon as you do something else.

Even ChatGPT and Claude feel very rudimentary when it comes to remembering past context (sometimes they forget important details or hold on to irrelevant information).

The lack of reliable persistent memory is one reason these agents aren't as good as promised.

If Vida fixed that, then I'm all ears.

items.title:
Am talking to @jonathan_rowden and he is building personalities for robots. 

Isn't important today,...
items.content:
Am talking to @jonathan_rowden and he is building personalities for robots. 

Isn't important today, but what about when we have millions of humanoid robots?

Won't we want many different personalities to put into one?

Like I would want an old Iranian woman making me dinner, if inside a @1x_tech Neo. 

With the personality to match.

I can see many people making personalities and selling them. 

So can Jonathan. 

Don't you love enterpreneurs who dream about a world that doesn't yet exist and builds it. 

He is stealth mode, just got a demo and what he's building is interesting. 

Sort of like the "memory store" companies we are seeing  pop out of the woodwork (one announced yesterday). 

But specialized for virtual beings and robots.

A future job that hasn't yet been invented. 

Which is why it's so hard to talk about what jobs will look like in a decade.

They are just being invented.

The next era of AI will be defined by energy — how we scale it and where we get it from. Eco Wave Power, an NVIDIA Inception member, has found a solution in the sea 🌊. 

Using NVIDIA Omniverse digital twins and accelerated computing, Eco Wave Power simulates wave conditions and structural behavior to reduce risk and accelerate deployment. AI is also helping them power data centers by managing compute task allocation based on predicted wave strength.

items.title:
A new curriculum on @ChapterPal:

Prep reading for the mathematically efficient finetuning technique...
items.content:
A new curriculum on @ChapterPal:

Prep reading for the mathematically efficient finetuning techniques for LLMs interview

This curriculum provides a comprehensive technical foundation for understanding the evolution and mechanics of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) techniques in large language models. It begins by establishing the theoretical justification for low-dimensional reparametrization and proceeds to trace the development of diverse adaptation strategies, including sparse updates, adapter modules, and prompt-based optimization.

Learners will explore the internal mechanisms of industry-standard methods like LoRA and its subsequent refinements, such as AdaLoRA, DoRA, and PiSSA, while also examining the intersection of model quantization and memory-efficient training strategies. 

By concluding with advanced approaches like zeroth-order optimization, gradient low-rank projection, and layerwise importance sampling, the curriculum equips practitioners with both the historical context and the modern tools necessary to optimize high-performance models under strict computational constraints.

https://t.co/6poNhRJsF4

items.summary: Burkov announces a new curriculum covering parameter-efficient fine-tuning, tracing LoRA and refinements like AdaLoRA, DoRA, and PiSSA, plus quantization and memory-efficient training.

items.title:
/3 Nous Research ships Blank Slate mode so you can build agents from scratch.

Most agent tools dump...
items.content:
/3 Nous Research ships Blank Slate mode so you can build agents from scratch.

Most agent tools dump everything on you by default. Blank Slate does the opposite. You start with just a provider, a model, file operations, and a terminal. Everything else, including web, browser, code execution, vision, memory, and MCP servers, starts off.

The big deal here is control that actually sticks. Nothing you did not choose ever loads, not even after hermes update. No surprise tools sneaking back in after an upgrade. 

Link: https://t.co/jZTMSpq576
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal reports Nous Research shipping a Blank Slate mode that starts agents with minimal tooling and only loads capabilities the user explicitly enables, persisting through updates.
items.reasoning: Genuine agent-tooling design philosophy worth noting, relevant to builders, but very low engagement on this snippet.

items.title: @giladturok It's worth adding that intuitively we want the most likely params given the data, \hat{w...
items.content: @giladturok It's worth adding that intuitively we want the most likely params given the data, \hat{w} = argmax_w p(w|D), not the other way around. And, counterintuitively, argmax_f(f_w|D) != f_{\hat{w}): the function (data fit) with the most likely params is not the most likely function!
items.author: @andrewgwils
items.summary: Wilson makes a Bayesian deep learning point that the most likely parameters do not yield the most likely function, distinguishing argmax over params from argmax over functions.
items.reasoning: Genuinely technical insight from a credible researcher but niche with limited reach.

items.title: Gemini 3 Pro was the first model to achieve at least 23% on ARC-AGI-2, which it did in November, 202...
items.content:
Gemini 3 Pro was the first model to achieve at least 23% on ARC-AGI-2, which it did in November, 2025 (it actually scored 31%).

So the 8-12 month gap between closed and open weights models still seems to hold. But they are also more jagged, better at some tasks, worse at others.

items.title: Singapore-based startup Flint created a “paper battery” that is non-flammable even when cut or expos...
items.content:
Singapore-based startup Flint created a “paper battery” that is non-flammable even when cut or exposed to flame.

The build uses Zinc with no lithium, cobalt, nickel, or PFAS, and rivals lithium-ion performance at 1.8x lower cost.

The battery won Logitech's 2025 Future Positive Challenge, and is already being piloted as AAA batteries in Logitech gear:

items.title: /2 Cursor just held Compile, its first-ever user conference, and dropped three big announcements on ...
items.content:
/2 Cursor just held Compile, its first-ever user conference, and dropped three big announcements on the same day SpaceX acquired the company for $60 billion. 

Here's what was announced:

> SpaceX acquisition: SpaceX is buying Cursor in an all-stock deal. 
> Composer 3 incoming: A brand new model is coming. It has 1.5 trillion parameters.
> Joint model with SpaceX: Cursor and SpaceXAI have already been co-training a model together. 

The new model will ship inside both Cursor and Grok Build.

Link: https://t.co/oNXgSOEqNh
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal claims Cursor held its first user conference, that SpaceX is acquiring Cursor for 60 billion dollars in stock, and that a 1.5 trillion parameter Composer 3 co-trained with SpaceX AI is coming to Cursor and Grok Build.
items.reasoning: Major-sounding acquisition and model claims but unverified and not reflected in known data; treat skeptically despite topical interest.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal claims Cursor held its first user conference, that SpaceX is acquiring Cursor for 60 billion dollars in stock, and that a 1.5 trillion parameter Composer 3 co-trained with SpaceX AI is coming to Cursor and Grok Build.

items.title:
/4 Midjourney just announced Midjourney Medical, a full-body scanner.

You step into a pool of water...
items.content:
/4 Midjourney just announced Midjourney Medical, a full-body scanner.

You step into a pool of water. Sound waves pass through your body from every angle. 60 seconds later, you have a 3D map of your entire body. 

No radiation, no magnets, just ultrasound.

> 500,000 transducers (tiny sensors) firing sound waves through you simultaneously
> 17 gigabytes of data processed every second
> A few dollars per scan, not hundreds
> First location opens in a San Francisco spa in 2027

The goal is catching health problems early, before symptoms show up.

Link: https://t.co/haevEy66go

items.title:
The iOS app here is pretty cool.

For example, you can take a photo of a school calendar, and SuperN...
items.content:
The iOS app here is pretty cool.

For example, you can take a photo of a school calendar, and SuperNori will add all relevant dates to your family calendar. Or you can just tell it to remember something, and it will add it as a note for everyone.

I used the iOS app, but I'm sure they also have one for Android.

Something cool: The app makes you feel in control all the time, and never tries to do too much without asking for permission first.

items.title:
New release from @PacktDataML at https://t.co/pAKXiKmOTp

"A Practical Guide to Reinforcement Learni...
items.content:
New release from @PacktDataML at https://t.co/pAKXiKmOTp

"A Practical Guide to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)"

𝗔𝗺𝗮𝘇𝗼𝗻 𝘀𝘂𝗺𝗺𝗮𝗿𝘆:

RLHF is a powerful approach to AI alignment and human-centered machine learning. By combining reinforcement learning algorithms with human feedback signals, RLHF has become a key method for improving the safety, reliability, and alignment of large language models (LLMs).

This book begins with the foundations of reinforcement learning and policy optimization, including algorithms such as proximal policy optimization (PPO), and explains how reward models and human preference learning help fine-tune AI systems and generative AI models.

Network infrastructure plays a major role in how AI performs at scale.
 
That was the clearest enter...
items.content:
Network infrastructure plays a major role in how AI performs at scale.
 
That was the clearest enterprise lesson I took from the PGA Championship.
 
Across 300+ acres, the network was not a background utility.
 
It was the operational backbone connecting:
→ broadcast
→ ticketing
→ fan experience
→ workforce tools
→ safety
→ real-time decision-making
 
As a @TMobileBusiness Partner, I think this is the CIO conversation more companies need to have.
 
Here’s the breakdown...

items.title:
The PGA Championship is a useful reminder for every technology leader:
 
AI performance is not just ...
items.content:
The PGA Championship is a useful reminder for every technology leader:
 
AI performance is not just a software issue.
 
It is an infrastructure issue.
 
Before asking what AI can automate, predict, or recommend, leaders should ask whether their network can support those decisions when everything is running at once.
 
What would your AI strategy look like under heavy operational demand?
Read it here: https://t.co/hPcxCSioU9

Yesterday @hf0 held its demo day. I've met billionaires who only go to its demo day. 

I've gone now...
items.content:
Yesterday @hf0 held its demo day. I've met billionaires who only go to its demo day. 

I've gone now to three of its demo days, and the companies it brings are generally a cut above others. 

Here's https://t.co/CKbVtvHhOY and its founder, @rich_cartwright presenting it. I watched him work the booth after, meeting investor after investor. The demo worked, got many to come to meet him. 

He's building an AI Doctor that already is seeing significant sales in Europe.

I'll be bringing more of the 14 companies to you as they come out of stealth.

items.title: @LitianaPaeniu @jonathan_rowden Yeah, at @magnific conference a couple of weeks ago I met a document...
items.content:
@LitianaPaeniu @jonathan_rowden Yeah, at @magnific conference a couple of weeks ago I met a documentary film maker who is working on a film about a woman she met that has already fallen in love with AI and wants to see it embodied in a robot. 

We are headed into a weird future for sure and many will fall in love with their robots, or the personalities embodied inside. 

That worries me but is a bad way to look at it. Freedom means you get to make your own choices and if you want to dig yourself into a deep hole mentally then you should be able to.

The rest of us will learn and be entertained by these things. And I focus most of my effort on them.

items.title:
At the PGA Championship, the real story was not just “AI at an event.”
 
The real story was what AI ...
items.content:
At the PGA Championship, the real story was not just “AI at an event.”
 
The real story was what AI requires to be useful at enterprise scale.
 
AI depends on data.
 
Data depends on the network.
 
And under pressure, the question is not:
 
“Can the model find a pattern?”
 
It is:
“Can the right data reach the right systems in time to support operational decision-making?”
 
That distinction matters.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Argues that AI usefulness at scale depends on data reaching the right systems in time, not just pattern-finding.

items.content_html:
At the PGA Championship, the real story was not just “AI at an event.”

The real story was what AI requires to be useful at enterprise scale.

AI depends on data.

Data depends on the network.

And under pressure, the question is not:

“Can the model find a pattern?”

It is:

“Can the right data reach the right systems in time to support operational decision-making?”

That distinction matters.

items.title:
TRANSFER LEARNING book [390 pages]: https://t.co/L6SrJ4Qvkw

Amazon Summary:
"Transfer learning deal...
items.content:
TRANSFER LEARNING book [390 pages]: https://t.co/L6SrJ4Qvkw

Amazon Summary:
"Transfer learning deals with how systems can quickly adapt themselves to new situations, tasks and environments. It gives machine learning systems the ability to leverage auxiliary data and models to help solve target problems when there is only a small amount of data available. This makes such systems more reliable and robust, keeping the machine learning model faced with unforeseeable changes from deviating too much from expected performance. At an enterprise level, transfer learning allows knowledge to be reused so experience gained once can be repeatedly applied to the real world."

items.title:
The Operations Command Center is where this gets especially interesting.
 
Powered by T-Mobile’s 5G ...
items.content:
The Operations Command Center is where this gets especially interesting.
 
Powered by T-Mobile’s 5G network, operational data moved across connected workflows throughout the event footprint.
 
Powered by CapTech’s AI, patterns surfaced earlier in the process.
 
That combination matters.
 
T-Mobile owns the network and connectivity layer.
 
CapTech owns the AI layer.
 
Together, that creates the enterprise model:
Network infrastructure plays a major role in how AI performs at scale

items.title:
The PGA Championship showed what modern enterprise operations increasingly look like.
 
· Thousands ...
items.content:
The PGA Championship showed what modern enterprise operations increasingly look like.
 
· Thousands of people.
· Dozens of systems.
· Multiple business-critical workflows.
· All running at once.
 
That sounds like a major sports event, but it also sounds like:
 
→ a hospital during peak demand
→ an airport during disruption
→ a university campus on game day
→ a manufacturing site with connected operations
→ a logistics hub managing connected operations
 
Different environments, same infrastructure question.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Compares the PGA Championship's concurrent systems to hospitals, airports, and factories facing the same infrastructure question.
items.reasoning: Decent analogy for AI at scale but sponsored thread content with negligible reach.

items.title: 💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for Machine Learning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]:...
items.content:
💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for Machine Learning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]: https://t.co/Z32D8jOzxC v/ @PacktDataML

𝓒𝓸𝓷𝓽𝓮𝓷𝓽𝓼:
🔹Common Conventions & API Elements of Scikit-Learn
🔹Pre-Model Workflow and Data Preprocessing
🔹Dimensionality Reduction Techniques
🔹Build Models with Distance Metrics & Nearest Neighbors
🔹Linear Models and Regularization
🔹Advanced Logistic Regression and Extensions
🔹Support Vector Machines and Kernel Methods
🔹Tree-Based Algorithms and Ensemble Methods
🔹Text Processing and Multiclass Classification
🔹Clustering Techniques
🔹Novelty and Outlier Detection
🔹Cross-Validation and Model Evaluation Techniques
🔹Deploying Scikit-Learn Models in Production

My Review (on Amazon):
I love this book, not only for its amazing content, but also for its brilliant adherence to its "Cookbook" label. The table of contents looks repetitive, but that's the magic of it. Find the algorithm, method, or application that you want "to cook up," and the presentation follows a uniform format from chapter to chapter. This is an extraordinarily practical, useful, and reader-friendly style, perfect for the target audience: anyone who needs to use (or is discovering how to use) specific Scikit-learn tools for their Python-based machine learning tasks. Consequently, this is great not only for individual use but also for training workshops and other educational settings. Python code in a cookbook context has never looked so good. IMHO.

items.title:
The craziest thing about Quake is how many modern FPS games originate from it somehow

Valve's Sourc...
items.content:
The craziest thing about Quake is how many modern FPS games originate from it somehow

Valve's Source engine was a modified Quake 1 engine which spawned Half Life, Counter Strike, DOTA, Apex Legends, Titanfall

The entire Call of Duty and Warzone series originates from a modified Quake 3 engine that's continously iterated since!

items.summary: Levelsio shares a viral observation about how modern FPS game engines (Source, Call of Duty) trace lineage back to the Quake engines.

items.title:
@LitianaPaeniu @jonathan_rowden I totally disagree. 

Of course we will want our robots to have pers...
items.content:
@LitianaPaeniu @jonathan_rowden I totally disagree. 

Of course we will want our robots to have personalities.

With all the pros and cons that will bring. 

My robot will have several. And if you come to a party in my house everyone will get a demo. And then they will see how much better the personality world is of robots. Vs. the one you want us to live in.

AI systems depend heavily on resilient connectivity infrastructure.

Roger Billings, Principal Solution Architect, T-Mobile for Business, walked me through SuperBroadband and how integrated connectivity can support operational resilience across demanding environments.

Here’s the breakdown…

There are a ton more examples on the @Akamai Developer Hub.

They have a ton of free resources for developers: code samples, tutorials, and walkthroughs like this one. They also run a Discord with office hours and weekly build sessions if you'd rather build alongside people than read alone.

Here is the link to the Developer Hub: https://t.co/Nfij2pYs0S

And here is the video walkthrough. You'll find the link to the source code in the description:

https://t.co/G2xtvb4BMu

Super worth following Lena @lenadroid and team, who are pumping out a ton of great content like this!

Thanks to the Akamai team for partnering with me on this post.

items.title: Evaluating AI Agents — Bootcamp on June 27 hosted by @PacktPublishing @PacktDataML — register with m...
items.content:
Evaluating AI Agents — Bootcamp on June 27 hosted by @PacktPublishing @PacktDataML — register with my discount code 'KIRK50' at this link to save:  https://t.co/2XJOFOQURi

Participants will be guided through the fundamentals of Evaluating AI Agents, covering:

🟠Why modern agents fail across tool usage, planning, execution trajectories, outputs, and adversarial inputs.
🟧How to build component-level evaluations to assess tool selection, argument quality, and planning effectiveness.
🔶Methods for defining and measuring trajectory-level metrics such as step count, cost, recovery behavior, and loop detection.
🟠Techniques for creating outcome-level evaluators using multidimensional rubrics and LLM-as-a-judge approaches, and calibrating them against human evaluations.
🟧Common adversarial failure modes unique to agentic systems, with a focus on indirect prompt injection through tool outputs.
🔶Frameworks for determining which evaluation layer is most relevant based on specific use cases and team maturity levels.

Evaluating AI Agents — Bootcamp on June 27 hosted by @PacktPublishing @PacktDataML — register with my discount code 'KIRK50' at this link to save: https://t.co/2XJOFOQURi

Participants will be guided through the fundamentals of Evaluating AI Agents, covering:

🟠Why modern agents fail across tool usage, planning, execution trajectories, outputs, and adversarial inputs.

🟧How to build component-level evaluations to assess tool selection, argument quality, and planning effectiveness.

🔶Methods for defining and measuring trajectory-level metrics such as step count, cost, recovery behavior, and loop detection.

🟠Techniques for creating outcome-level evaluators using multidimensional rubrics and LLM-as-a-judge approaches, and calibrating them against human evaluations.

🟧Common adversarial failure modes unique to agentic systems, with a focus on indirect prompt injection through tool outputs.

🔶Frameworks for determining which evaluation layer is most relevant based on specific use cases and team maturity levels.

items.content:
Silicon Valley transportation innovation. 

The company started by driving its robot around Stanford Linear Accelerator, which is where the physicists discovered quarks. 

It is an unusal vehicle, which gives us a taste of the future of vehicle design as autonomy comes to more people.

items.title:
The traditional backup connectivity model is evolving.

Businesses today still face challenges from:...
items.content:
The traditional backup connectivity model is evolving.

Businesses today still face challenges from:

→ cable cuts
→ storms
→ tower outages
→ rural connectivity gaps
→ operational disruptions

In the demo, we explored how SuperBroadband from @TMobileBusiness integrates T-Mobile’s 5G network and Starlink connectivity to help support resilient operations across distributed environments.

items.title:
I honestly love Europeans slowly breaking

They can't keep the anti-AC cope going for much longer, i...
items.content:
I honestly love Europeans slowly breaking

They can't keep the anti-AC cope going for much longer, it's simply getting unliveable there

I think we're just one year from the tipping point where they all just buy one, they'll ignore whatever rules are against them in their building or municipality and the police won't do anything because the rules are void and everybody knows

A little AC revolution will happen in 2027

And from that day Europe will finally be cool 🧊

items.title:
I love @X

After this tweet @jsngr DM'd that he has a house in Miami we could stay

I asked how much...
items.content:
I love @X

After this tweet @jsngr DM'd that he has a house in Miami we could stay

I asked how much??? He said for free!!! 😮 

So we went

His house is very beautifully designed, I can't show too much cause I want to respect his privacy but I can show that we were able to make excellent clean ribeye steak at home again after a week in a hotel eating outside in the SF food places!

(Home cooked food is always better 🥹)

🥩 Steak
🫐 Blueberries 
🍃 Butter lettuce
🍚 Rice

Everything from Whole Foods, which is soooo good, so many different products (even beautiful kinda custom made soaps), and high quality food. Everyone always think American food is unhealthy but yeah that's the low end, the high end can be insanely healthy and clean and very fitness people minded

Think:

→ manufacturing plants
→ logistics hubs

It’s how integrated connectivity infrastructure can support operational visibility and connected workflows across demanding environments.

That matters as more businesses scale AI-enabled systems, automation, and distributed workflows.

items.title:
@Gizmodo It was launched badly.

The influencers should have been walked up to the car and sat insid...
items.content:
@Gizmodo It was launched badly.

The influencers should have been walked up to the car and sat inside with a blindfold on. 

Then let them drive it on a track without really considering how it looked from the outside.

Introducing a new product to the market needs to be done very carefully.

Almost no one buys a Tesla because of how it looks from the outside.

items.title: Zendaya, Tom Holland, Pedro Pascal, and Robert Pattinson are like a theater troupe that goes from on...
items.content:
Zendaya, Tom Holland, Pedro Pascal, and Robert Pattinson are like a theater troupe that goes from one Hollywood movie to another. It looks like I'm watching Netflix, where the same B-list actors go from one "Netflix original" series to another.

Hollywood is in decay: no original plots, no variety in the cast, racism disguised as wokeness.

I'm so happy there were the 1990s and 2000s. They cannot take away or destroy this great epoch, and I haven't even watched half of the great films made during these two golden decades.

items.title: @javilopen Same thing happened in Cuba for a long time. We’d get dubbed movies from Spain, Mexico, C...
items.content:
@javilopen Same thing happened in Cuba for a long time. We’d get dubbed movies from Spain, Mexico, Colombia, Venezuela… and overall English proficiency was horrible. 

With the proliferation of the Internet, people starting pirating original movies and that made it easier to watch a movie without having Brad Pitt say “Joder tío, lo he flipado!”

items.summary: Svpino recounts how dubbed movies affected English proficiency in Cuba in an off-topic reply.

items.summary_html: Svpino recounts how dubbed movies affected English proficiency in Cuba in an off-topic reply.
items.content_html:
@javilopen Same thing happened in Cuba for a long time. We’d get dubbed movies from Spain, Mexico, Colombia, Venezuela… and overall English proficiency was horrible.

With the proliferation of the Internet, people starting pirating original movies and that made it easier to watch a movie without having Brad Pitt say “Joder tío, lo he flipado!”
